question: The 'plan-to-produce' (P2P) process covers:
options: Only the manufacturing step in a production operation

The complete cycle from demand planning through production execution: Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP — aligning sales forecasts with production capacity); Master Production Scheduling (which products to make, in what quantities, and when); Material Requirements Planning (MRP — what materials are needed, when to order them); Capacity Planning (do we have enough machines, labor, and floor space?); Production Execution (actual manufacturing, quality control, work-in-process tracking); and Inventory Management (finished goods storage, replenishment planning); the process is cross-functional: Sales provides demand forecasts; Finance approves capital investments; Operations executes; Procurement sources materials; the plan-to-produce process directly determines: production costs, delivery performance, inventory levels, and capacity utilization — the core operational performance drivers
Only activities performed inside the factory
Only the inventory planning and warehousing functions
explanation: Plan-to-produce and the S&OP process: Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP) is the governance process where sales forecasts, production plans, and inventory targets are aligned across functions. Without S&OP, sales might promise customers delivery dates that operations can't meet; or operations might build inventory that sales can't sell. The monthly S&OP cycle: (1) Statistical demand review (2-3 year forecast); (2) Demand review (sales adjustments + demand signals); (3) Supply review (operations capacity check); (4) Pre-S&OP (resolve gaps between demand and supply); (5) Executive S&OP (leadership alignment on the plan). Toyota's production system is the global benchmark for plan-to-produce excellence: zero waste (muda), just-in-time production, and built-in quality (jidoka) principles have been studied and imitated globally.

question: A consumer electronics company produces 200,000 units/month of a single product. Actual demand averages 180,000 units/month but varies: in the low month it's 140,000; in the high month it's 220,000. Management must choose a production strategy: (A) Level production at 200,000/month; (B) Chase demand — adjust production to match actual demand monthly; (C) Hybrid — level at 170,000/month and use overtime/subcontracting for demand above that. What are the trade-offs of each strategy?
options: Level production is always optimal because it minimizes unit production costs

Each strategy optimizes different costs with specific trade-offs: Level production (200,000/month): Benefits: stable workforce (no hiring/firing costs), maximum equipment utilization, predictable unit costs; Costs: excess inventory in low months (140,000 demand = 60,000 units unsold = storage and carrying cost), risk of obsolescence if product changes, capital tied up in inventory; works best for: non-perishable goods, stable workforce, high fixed costs vs. variable. Chase production (match demand): Benefits: no excess inventory, no carrying costs, production matches demand perfectly; Costs: highly variable workforce (layoffs and rehires are expensive and damaging to morale), variable equipment utilization (expensive idle capacity in low months), scheduling complexity; works best for: perishable goods, seasonal demand, variable-cost-heavy operations, high inventory carrying costs. Hybrid (170K level + flex): Benefits: some workforce stability, manageable inventory levels, overtime for peaks is more flexible than hiring; Costs: overtime premium cost (1.5x regular wage), subcontracting risk (quality control, lead time), some carrying cost; Most companies use hybrid — level at a base rate, use overtime and subcontracting for peaks
Chase production always minimizes costs because it eliminates inventory
Production planning has no effect on labor costs or workforce management
explanation: Production strategy real examples: Toyota's Toyota Production System (TPS) achieves a form of level production through 'heijunka' (production leveling) — spreading the production of different products evenly across time to smooth demand variability. Instead of making 100 of model A in the morning and 100 of model B in the afternoon, Toyota makes alternating units across the day. This allows just-in-time supply chain management (suppliers deliver in small, frequent batches rather than large weekly batches). Fashion retail's chase strategy: Zara (Inditex) uses a chase production model — producing small batches, testing demand in stores, and producing more of what's selling. This is the operational basis of 'fast fashion' — small batches, rapid replenishment, no excess inventory of any single item. The inventory carrying cost calculation: inventory carrying cost = 20-30% of inventory value annually (includes: capital cost, storage, insurance, obsolescence risk, handling). For $50M in inventory, carrying cost = $10-15M/year — a significant argument for chase or hybrid production strategies.

question: Toyota's 'just-in-time' (JIT) production system delivers components to the production line exactly when needed — minutes before use rather than days of buffer inventory. When a Japanese earthquake (2011) disrupted Toyota's supply chain and halted production for weeks, critics said JIT was 'fragile.' Toyota responded that the system worked as designed. Who is right?
options: The critics are correct that JIT is fundamentally incompatible with supply chain resilience

The critics' objection highlights a genuine risk; Toyota's response reveals a more nuanced picture: JIT design philosophy: Toyota's original JIT was designed for a geographically concentrated supply chain (most suppliers within 50km of Toyota assembly plants in Japan); disruptions would affect all manufacturers equally (a Japan earthquake would halt production regardless of inventory level — earthquake also damages the factory); inventory would not have prevented the shutdown. The 2011 earthquake specific analysis: (1) Toyota's factories were damaged (can't produce regardless of inventory); (2) Many suppliers were also in the earthquake zone — having 90 days of inventory doesn't help if the component supplier's factory is destroyed; (3) The recovery: Toyota's supply chain expertise meant they could assess supplier damage, redirect procurement, and restart production faster than competitors with more inventory; their supply chain visibility (knowing exactly which suppliers, which parts) enabled faster recovery. The genuine vulnerability: JIT is vulnerable to geographic concentration risk — a Japan-only earthquake hitting Japan-concentrated supply chains. Post-2011, Toyota diversified more supply to multiple countries. The COVID supply chain crisis: semiconductor shortages that began 2021 hit Toyota harder than some competitors because semiconductor inventory had been reduced under JIT assumptions — a case where JIT logic genuinely created vulnerability
JIT production always creates catastrophic supply chain risk that outweighs its benefits
Supply chain disruption risk is identical regardless of inventory level
explanation: JIT and supply chain resilience post-COVID: the 2021 semiconductor shortage caused Toyota and other automakers to halt production lines despite massive demand backlog. The shortage occurred because: automotive semiconductor demand had been reduced during COVID (supply contracted); when automotive demand rebounded faster than expected, semiconductor manufacturers couldn't quickly expand capacity; automakers with JIT inventory policies didn't have buffer stock. The lesson: JIT assumes supplier can always respond quickly; semiconductor fabrication takes 6-9 months to expand — a mismatch with JIT's 'on demand' assumption. The Toyota inventory strategy update: post-COVID, Toyota moved to maintain 2-6 months of inventory for 'high risk' components (semiconductors, rare earth elements) while maintaining JIT for bulk commodities (steel, rubber) where supply is more reliable. This 'risk-stratified' inventory approach — JIT where supplier responsiveness is high; safety stock where it's low — is the emerging consensus for supply chain design after COVID disruptions.

question: Amazon's fulfillment center network uses a 'chaotic storage' system: products are not organized by category (all electronics together, all books together) — instead, products are placed in any available bin and the system records the exact bin location. This creates visually chaotic warehouses but optimizes operational performance. What is the logic of this approach?
options: Chaotic storage is inefficient and Amazon would perform better with organized category storage

Algorithmic inventory management outperforms human-intuitive organization when computer tracking enables optimization: the traditional warehouse organization logic: organized by category so humans can find items; the problem: high-demand items (top 1,000 SKUs) are clustered in the same area, creating congestion around popular items; workers walking to fulfill orders travel long distances to reach the appropriate category sections; the chaotic storage logic: (1) The picking algorithm is optimized: when an order is placed, the algorithm identifies the bin location for each item; the picking path is calculated to minimize travel time; multiple orders are batched into single picking trips; (2) Congestion elimination: popular items are distributed randomly throughout the facility — no single area becomes a bottleneck; (3) Space utilization: any bin can hold any product; no category zones means no wasted space from a category area that's oversized for current inventory; (4) The enabling technology: the system works only if every item's location is precisely tracked in real-time; barcodes and now computer vision enable this at scale; (5) The Kiva robot extension: Amazon's Kiva robots (now Amazon Robotics) bring storage pods to stationary workers — workers don't walk at all; chaotic storage enables each worker to pick from many different product pods
Warehouse organization has no effect on labor productivity
Chaotic storage requires workers to memorize large amounts of inventory locations
explanation: Amazon's warehouse productivity: Amazon's fulfillment centers process 300+ items per hour per worker (vs. 100 for traditional warehouses). The chaotic storage system is a key contributor. The Kiva robot acquisition (2012, $775M): Amazon bought Kiva Systems, maker of mobile fulfillment robots, and renamed it Amazon Robotics. The Kiva pods are storage units on wheels that robots move to stationary workers — the workers scan items and pack orders without walking. The chaotic storage + robot model creates: 50% increase in storage density (no aisle navigation required when robots move the pods), 50-60% reduction in labor per unit, and faster order cycle time. The Amazon Robotics expansion: Amazon has deployed 750,000+ robots across its fulfillment centers as of 2023, representing the largest robotics deployment in commercial history. The operations management lesson: optimizing for human intuition (organized storage that 'makes sense' visually) can be suboptimal when algorithms can manage complexity that exceeds human tracking capability.

question: A hospital is applying lean manufacturing principles (originally from Toyota) to its emergency department. The ED has a 4-hour average patient wait time. A lean team identifies: patients wait 45 minutes for registration paperwork (happens before triage); blood tests take 2 hours but the actual analysis is 12 minutes (waiting time is the issue); doctors see the patient for 10 minutes then write orders, but the orders are fulfilled an hour later due to queuing at the pharmacy and imaging departments. What lean tools apply to these problems?
options: Lean manufacturing principles only apply to assembly line manufacturing and have no healthcare relevance

Value stream mapping identifies waste (muda) and lean tools address each type: the lean analysis: the patient journey from arrival to disposition is the 'value stream'; every minute a patient waits without receiving care is 'waste' (non-value-added time). Registration waiting time (45 min): cause = sequential process (arrive → wait → register → wait → triage); waste type = waiting muda; lean solution = parallel processing (a 'rapid triage' system does a quick triage assessment immediately on arrival, while registration can happen simultaneously or later; patients with urgent needs bypass registration entirely). Blood test waiting (2 hours, 12 minutes actual): cause = batch processing (tests are run in batches when enough samples accumulate) + transport delays; waste type = waiting + transport muda; lean solution = point-of-care testing (test analyzers in the ED that provide results in 30 minutes; higher unit cost per test but faster throughput; bottleneck analysis shows the hour saved in waiting has more value than the higher reagent cost per test). Order fulfillment queuing: cause = sequential queuing at pharmacy and imaging; waste type = queuing + waiting; lean solution = priority protocols (ED orders have a separate queue with faster processing); 5S (organize pharmacy workspace so ED medications are immediately accessible without full pharmacy workflow)
Lean principles always reduce quality when applied to healthcare
Reducing wait time in an ED always requires adding staff or facilities
explanation: Lean healthcare evidence: Virginia Mason Medical Center (Seattle) implemented Toyota's Production System in healthcare, reducing patient wait times, medication errors, and cost simultaneously — winning national quality awards. The Virginia Mason application: 'kaizen events' (rapid improvement workshops) applied to specific processes (the medication process, the check-in process, OR scheduling) reduced waste identified through value stream mapping. The point-of-care testing example: adding a blood analyzer in the ED at a higher cost-per-test than the central lab produces a measurably faster patient throughput — the throughput value (faster bed availability, better patient outcomes) exceeds the higher unit cost of the test. This is a cross-functional economic decision: the ED wants the faster test; the lab resists (their cost center absorbs the higher-cost equipment); the CFO needs to see the system-wide economics to approve. The systems view: lean in healthcare requires optimizing across departmental boundaries (ED, pharmacy, lab) rather than each department optimizing its own metrics — exactly the cross-functional coordination challenge that lean management addresses.

question: Intel's semiconductor fabrication plants ('fabs') have 2-3 year lead times to build, cost $20B+ each, and once built operate 24/7 for 20+ years. Demand for chips fluctuates significantly (tech cycles, smartphone upgrade cycles, AI boom/bust). How does this mismatch between capacity commitment timeline and demand volatility create strategic challenges?
options: Intel should build new fabs only when demand certainty is fully confirmed

Long-duration capacity commitment under demand uncertainty creates strategic inflection points: the structural problem: Intel must decide 2-3 years in advance what capacity to build for a market that can change dramatically in 18 months; once built, the fab's fixed cost structure (depreciation, labor, maintenance) is ~$3-4B/year regardless of utilization — you can't 'turn off' a semiconductor fab during demand downturns. The strategic options: (1) Build aggressively (capacity ahead of demand): risk of overcapacity during downturns (destroys margins); benefit: wins market share when demand is high because capacity is available; Intel's historical strategy; (2) Build conservatively (capacity behind demand): risk of losing market share during demand spikes (can't supply); benefit: higher utilization rates and margins during normal times; Samsung's strategy in commodity memory chips; (3) Use contract manufacturing (TSMC model): separate design (Intel, Qualcomm, NVIDIA) from manufacturing (TSMC, Samsung Foundry) — chipless design companies ('fabless') like NVIDIA can scale demand without building fabs; TSMC amortizes fab cost across many customers; (4) Government partnership: US CHIPS Act provides $52B in subsidies for fab construction — reducing the private financial risk of long-duration investment; why: national security rationale (Taiwan concentration risk for US semiconductor supply) overrides pure market economics. The Intel strategic challenge (2020s): Intel fell behind TSMC in process technology (Intel's 7nm equivalent lagged TSMC's 5nm commercial production); the catch-up requires $100B+ in new fab investment under genuine technology uncertainty
Semiconductor capacity planning has no strategic implications because demand is always predictable
Semiconductor fabs should be built only in the US regardless of cost considerations
explanation: Intel's capacity strategy challenges: Intel's 'tick-tock' model (alternating chip architecture improvements with process technology improvements) worked until ~2014 when Intel's process technology advancement slowed. TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company) became the world's leading fab, manufacturing chips for Apple, NVIDIA, AMD, and others. The concentration risk: 60%+ of world's most advanced chips are manufactured in Taiwan; if a Taiwan-China conflict disrupted TSMC, global technology production would be severely impacted — hence the US, EU, and Japan government subsidies to build advanced fabs in their territories. The TSMC Arizona fab: TSMC's planned Arizona fabs represent a significant geopolitical/strategic investment — at costs significantly higher than Taiwan equivalent capacity. The US CHIPS Act economics: Intel, TSMC, and Samsung each received ~$7-10B in grants and tax credits for US fab construction; the government subsidy reduces but doesn't eliminate the commercial risk. The capacity commitment uncertainty: the AI semiconductor boom (2023-2024) created unexpected demand for advanced chips (NVIDIA H100s) that TSMC couldn't immediately satisfy — illustrating that even the best capacity planning cannot perfectly predict demand 2-3 years out.

question: A food company's production planning team must decide: produce 2 million units of a seasonal product (holiday cookies) in October and November for December sales, or produce year-round in smaller batches. The product has a 6-month shelf life. Year-round production costs are 15% higher per unit (lower equipment utilization; more production changeovers). What factors should inform the production timing decision?
options: Seasonal products should always be produced seasonally immediately before the selling period

Multiple operational and financial factors determine the optimal timing: the cost comparison: seasonal production (produce 2M units in 2 months): production cost = base cost; carrying cost = average $2M inventory × 20% carrying rate × 2 months = $66K; stockout risk = if October/November production goes wrong, no time to recover before December sales; capacity requirement: production lines must process 1M units/month vs. year-round average of 167K/month = 6× peak capacity requirement; overtime and temporary labor costs. Year-round production (167K/month): production cost per unit = +15% (vs. seasonal); total additional cost on 2M units × $X/unit × 15%; more predictable staffing; no overtime; lower capacity peaks; shelf life risk: is 6-month shelf life sufficient for product made in January for December? (January → December = 11 months → exceeds shelf life). The shelf life constraint analysis: if the product has a 6-month shelf life, year-round production is constrained — product made in January can't be sold in December; the earliest production window is July. The practical decision: the 6-month shelf life limits year-round production to starting no earlier than July; the 15% cost premium on 2M units must be calculated vs. the savings from smoother production (lower overtime, lower capacity pressure); for many food companies, the 15% premium is justified by operational stability
Year-round production is always preferable to seasonal production
Shelf life constraints are irrelevant for production planning decisions
explanation: Seasonal production planning in food: the food industry faces this trade-off consistently. Cadbury (Creme Eggs) produces their Easter products year-round because the alternative — producing all eggs in a 6-week period — would require enormous peak capacity and result in inferior product quality. The capacity planning issue: if production must be completed in 2 months, the equipment, packaging lines, and workforce must be scaled to handle 6× the year-round rate — potentially requiring capital investment in additional equipment used only 2 months/year. The work-in-process and finished goods inventory: seasonal production often creates a 'production valley' — inventory builds in October/November, is sold in December, then inventory drops to near zero in January. This creates workforce management challenges (hiring/training temporary workers, then laying them off) and equipment utilization gaps. Campbell's Soup's production planning: Campbell's produces significant volumes of soup in summer for fall/winter sale — a form of counter-seasonal production that smooths factory utilization. The chicken-or-egg capacity question: if the demand forecasts are uncertain (will this holiday cookie product sell 1.5M or 2.5M units?), building the safety stock buffer early is more valuable than the carrying cost savings from waiting until the last moment.
